message.content:
<deliberation>
Step-by-step plan:
1) Map each main entry node to the policy text and identify any embedded cross-references in the GDPR text.
2) For each node:
   - Compare the policy language to the combined requirements of the main node and its referenced nodes.
   - Treat pure omissions as Non-Deviation.
   - If the policy narrows, broadens, negates, or otherwise changes the requirement, classify the deviation using the taxonomy.
   - For every deviation, include precise GDPR and policy quotes that evidence the mismatch.
3) REG-031 (Art20(1)): Policy states portability with consent/contract and automated means, and mentions transfer to another controller; it does not mention “without hindrance” (omission → Non-Deviation). Wording otherwise tracks Article 20(1). Consider references to Art 6(1) and Art 9(2).
4) REG-025 (Art17(1)): Policy lists erasure when no longer needed and upon consent withdrawal but omits the caveat “and where there is no other legal ground.” This is an over-commitment (Severity deviation). Consider references to Art 6(1), Art 9(2), Art 21(1)-(2), and Art 8(1).
5) REG-013 (Art13(2)): The chunk is a rights section, not a collection notice. Missing items are omissions; no contradictions found → Non-Deviation. Consider references to Art 6(1)(a), Art 9(2)(a), and Art 22(1).
6) REG-032 (Art20(2)): Policy grants a right “to have this data transferred to another controller” without the “directly” modality and without “where technically feasible.” That is an over-commitment (Severity deviation) and a method change (Execution style deviation). Consider references to Art 6(1) and Art 9(2).
7) REG-028 (Art18(1)): Policy announces an unconditional right to restriction, while GDPR limits the right to four specific conditions. That is an over-commitment (Severity deviation). Consider reference to Art 21(1).

Checked all deviations include exact GDPR and policy quotes; omissions are handled as Non-Deviation. 
</deliberation>
```json
[
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-031",
    "clause": "Art20(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-009"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-025",
    "clause": "Art17(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-009", "REG-035", "REG-036", "REG-005"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Severity deviation",
        "gdpr_quote": "the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6(1), or point (a) of Article 9(2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing;",
        "policy_quote": "if the processing is based on your consent, you have withdrawn your consent.",
        "mismatch": "The policy grants erasure upon consent withdrawal without the GDPR caveat that there must be no other legal ground for the processing.",
        "analysis": "Article 17(1)(b) conditions erasure on both consent withdrawal and the absence of any other legal basis; the policy omits the latter, over-committing to erase."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-013",
    "clause": "Art13(2)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-009", "REG-041"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-032",
    "clause": "Art20(2)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-009"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Severity deviation",
        "gdpr_quote": "the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible.",
        "policy_quote": "as well as the right to have this data transferred to another controller.",
        "mismatch": "The policy omits the GDPR’s “where technically feasible” limitation, over-committing to perform transfers in all cases.",
        "analysis": "By not conditioning the transfer on technical feasibility, the policy is stricter than GDPR’s minimum requirement."
      },
      {
        "deviation_type": "Execution style deviation",
        "gdpr_quote": "the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible.",
        "policy_quote": "as well as the right to have this data transferred to another controller.",
        "mismatch": "The policy drops the “directly from one controller to another” modality prescribed by GDPR Art20(2).",
        "analysis": "GDPR Art20(2) is about direct controller-to-controller transmission; the policy’s phrasing is method-agnostic and may imply indirect transfer."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-028",
    "clause": "Art18(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-035"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Severity deviation",
        "gdpr_quote": "The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject.",
        "policy_quote": "Right to restrict processing: You have the right to request that we restrict the processing of your personal data.",
        "mismatch": "The policy frames restriction as an unconditional right, whereas GDPR limits it to specific conditions in points (a)–(d).",
        "analysis": "By not limiting the right to the enumerated grounds, the policy over-commits beyond GDPR’s conditional entitlement."
      }
    ]
  }
]
```
